segments.text: Thermals — how do thermals form?
We have the sun, as we've already discussed — it's the engine of the weather.
The sun shines on the Earth's surface and warms the air layer near the ground.
Accordingly, there's a slightly warmer layer of air at the ground.
That's really important to understand for thermals.
When we're flying or looking for thermals, we always have to ask ourselves,
which air mass, or rather which ground, warms up more than the other.
It's always about a temperature difference.
It doesn't have to be warm per se — in winter we can also fly thermals.
If the forests, for example, are snow-free, they warm up more than a snow-covered meadow slope.
So the thermals are to be found over the forest.
So the temperature difference is always really important.
It also has to do with the albedo effect, because some surfaces warm up more strongly than others.
For example, there's a dry field that's been harvested.
It warms up more than a green meadow that maybe is still damp.
So you always have to ask which surface warms up more.
And there's also the basic rule — the more reflective a surface is,
snow for example, the less it actually warms up.
Or a black surface, a dark surface, of course warms up a lot more,
because it absorbs much more heat from the sun's rays.
Another thing that's really important for warming is the sun's angle of incidence.
Ideally, the sun's rays that we see here
hit a surface at a right angle.
That way the surface gets warmed much more strongly
than a surface that's tilted, where the sun's rays only hit at a shallow angle.
Think for example again of the polar regions and the tropical regions.
That also has to do only with the angle of incidence of the sun.
Accordingly, a warm air layer forms at the ground, which builds a bubble.
And the warm air layer, when it has enough temperature difference,
and is released by a small trigger,
detaches and starts to rise.
You can picture it a bit like a lava lamp.
This air mass stays closed.
That's again an air mass.
It's an air mass with a uniform property.
It's warmer than the air mass around it.
And it rises away.
So here we have two air masses.
We have the atmosphere outside, which has a certain temperature,
which we'll also briefly touch on.
And the warm air layer that rises.
The bubble that rises.
And forms the thermal.
You can also picture a hot-air balloon.
It also rises because it has warmer, warm air,
which is less dense.
And so the hot-air balloon rises.
The same is true of the thermal,
of this thermal bubble that rises.
Again, we have a certain air pressure
that prevails in our atmosphere.
We have higher air pressure in the lower region
than in the upper layers.
Now we come back to air pressure.
You can see, we're starting to tie things together.
Accordingly, when a balloon like this rises —
some of you may know this from diving.
Or with a weather balloon you see it very impressively.
When it's down low,
it has a very small volume of the gas inside it.
When it rises, where the air pressure here drops,
then the gas can expand more and more.
And so the volume increases,
of the air parcel that's rising.
And then we come to the cooling.
When the balloon rises,
there's a decompression,
the pressure of the rising air parcel drops.
It expands, the pressure drops.
And when in an air mass, or in a gas generally, the pressure drops,
then the temperature decreases.
You see this very impressively, for example,
with a compressed-air gun —
when you blow air out, it's ice cold.
That basically only has to do with the fact
that the compressed air from the compressor
expands very quickly and cools down as a result.
So an air mass that expands cools down.
Accordingly, the opposite is also true.
When an air mass is compressed,
it warms up.
You also see this in practice, for example,
when you pump up a tyre.
When you pump up a pressurised tyre,
at some point it gets really warm.
And that's because you're compressing a lot of air there.
Always keep this picture in mind.
It's really important for various meteorological phenomena,
including thermals here.
If you look at the numbers,
you can imagine
that the ground starts at 20 degrees.
These are just approximate numbers.
And then it rises
and the temperature decreases more and more with altitude.
Exactly.
That has to do with decompression
and with this air mass rising.
In the end, if we just picture it,
in the atmosphere we have
a certain temperature gradient.
Ideally it gets colder and colder with altitude.
The air mass here is sitting at night, for example.
At night the air mass flows here.
It's a bit warmer at ground level
than higher up.
On Mount Everest it's much colder
than down at the sea, for example,
just to picture it visually.
And there you have the temperature gradient,
which is again a progression,
a gradient,
a temperature gradient of this air mass,
which is just sitting here.
And now in the morning the sun comes up,
the engine of the weather starts to work,
starts to heat up the surfaces on the ground
and the thermals rise away
and cool down.
And through the cooling process,
the thermal usually
reaches a point somewhere
where it's just as cold
as the surrounding air.
Now here in this case, the thermal top
is reached at the point
where the air mass is at minus two degrees.
And the surrounding air,
where you're sitting right there,
is at a certain point
exactly as cold
as the parcel that's rising.
Accordingly,
the hot-air balloon no longer has any temperature advantage
and it won't rise any further.
The thermal stops at a certain point.
Exactly,
that has to do with the temperature state curve,
as it's also called.
The temperature state curve describes
really just the atmosphere,
that is, the stratification of the air mass,
as it sits there.
The cooling
that takes place here
is also described
as an adiabatic process
or as adiabatic cooling.
And there are two of them,
which I'll get into in a moment.
And you can imagine
that the surrounding air mass
has a certain state.
Here it's just shown a bit differently,
so you can picture
that there are two different air masses —
that's really important.
There's very little mixing going on between them.
And especially the way
you want to learn it for the theory exam,
that's the most relevant point.
There's also a Meteo Advanced course,
where we get into thermal theory in much more detail.
There are also other effects there
that are relevant,
but for the basic theory course
they go way too far.
Exactly,
now we see the temperature decrease
that makes the air parcel rise.
And there's a second factor that comes in,
which for us as pilots is really very important.
And that's humidity.
In an air parcel we have —
and right now in this room here too —
we have a certain proportion of water vapour.
So we don't only have oxygen and nitrogen,
but we also have a certain water-vapour content
in our air parcel.
Humidity,
it's variably moist.
Depending on
what kind of air mass it is.
You can imagine,
with these blue dots,
that's the water vapour in this gas.
And when the air parcel now rises
and cools down,
then the water vapour has less and less room,
you can picture it that way.
And at some point there's the change,
where the air mass can no longer
absorb any more moisture.
And then the gaseous water vapour starts
to change its state of matter into liquid.
Liquid is relative,
because we usually see it as clouds.
So every fog and every cloud is actually nothing other
than water in the air.
And really, really microscopically small water droplets.
It doesn't have to be rain;
it's really, really tiny droplets,
which we perceive,
for our eye, more like a gas.
But they're really water droplets in the air.
And then we talk about condensation.
The condensation level,
where the air mass starts to condense,
the water vapour that's in there.
A word we also hear again and again
is the relative humidity,
which then increases.
And at 100 percent of course we've reached the point
where the air mass can no longer absorb any more moisture.
Exactly.
Then, when the condensation process kicks in,
when the water vapour becomes water,
so when it really turns into tiny water droplets,
then physically a phenomenon happens,
namely that when it goes from gaseous to liquid,
heat is released.
A little bit of heat is released.
And of course also if it were to change from liquid
into the solid state.
Those are the three states of matter.
After that a little bit of heat is released,
a little bit of energy is released in this process.
The other thing is the opposite of course.
When we have a change of state
from solid to liquid or from liquid to gaseous,
then heat is consumed.
An important point in this condensation process
is the back-of-the-envelope calculation
we're just looking at here.
And that's important for you
to understand what's roughly happening here.
When the air mass rises, it cools down.
As long as it rises dry-adiabatically,
that is, without any condensation process taking place,
it's very simple.
It's just 1 degree per 100 metres.
So when it rises,
it cools down by 1 degree per 100 metres
in this thermal bubble.
That's very simple.
You can remember that number.
In the moist-adiabatic process
the back-of-the-envelope calculation starts.
Specifically, we have the 1 degree per 100 metres
by which it cools.
But working against that is the condensation process,
which always releases a bit of energy
in the form of heat.
And so here of course
a certain amount is subtracted
from those degrees per 100 metres,
which in the end comes out to roughly
0.65 degrees per 100 metres.
The value, as you can see here,
isn't always exactly the same.
That has to do with the fact
that a warm air mass
can absorb more moisture
than a cold one.
In a warm air mass we have correspondingly
more moisture that starts to condense
than in a colder air mass,
for example in polar regions.
So the value
varies a little.
But for you it's important to know
that it's simply smaller than 1 degree.
Here's the back-of-the-envelope calculation
of moist-adiabatic cooling.
You can remember these two numbers.
And now let's look at
what that means in practice.
Now we see the air mass
rising here below.
In this case a dry-adiabatic cooling
with 1 degree per 100 metres.
At a certain point
the rising air mass reaches
the condensation level.
The condensation process
begins.
And so the air mass cools
now, in this case, only by
0.65 degrees per 100 metres.
That's the moist-adiabatic process.
That keeps going
until, as we had before,
the air parcel has the same temperature
as the surrounding air.
Those are the two
adiabatic cooling processes
you need to know.
Then we have an image here maybe
from practice,
from flight practice.
You see the landscape.
You have to ask yourself: where does
the air mass rise best?
Or much more importantly: where does
the ground warm up the most?
That's right-angled sun exposure.
Maybe a ground that warms up a bit more
than the ground around it.
In this case the meadow areas
certainly warm up a bit more
than a forest.
On the south-facing slope, at some point the thermal lifts off.
It rises, it rises, it rises
and at some point it forms
beautiful cumulus clouds
that mark the thermals.
If of course we also
have wind on top of that,
if we picture a balloon
rising, then of course it drifts
into the wind, with the wind.
And so it's
a puzzle when you come here
for cross-country flying — where do I fly to?
You always have to piece together
where the thermal lifts off
and where it forms a nice little cloud.
Another important point:
the thermal, the thermal air mass
that rises, doesn't always have to
form a little cumulus cloud.
It can also be that the
temperature advantage of the thermal —
I'm thinking of the balloon — is exhausted,
so that it has equalised,
before a condensation process
takes place. Then over here
there might be exactly the same — over there
exactly the same thermal, but it has
stopped rising before
it condensed. And so
we talk about blue thermals.
That's the term you can remember.
Blue thermals are actually nothing other
than thermals that never condensed.
Right. A term for thermals
that you also hear from time to time
is the term lee thermal.
We had it before — windward
and leeward. The lee side,
the side of the mountain facing away from the wind.
If we picture a wind here,
this is the side facing away from the wind
of these mountains. Maybe we have
sun exposure that warms
exactly these surfaces,
the angle of the sun,
more than the other side.
And so the thermal lifts off best there.
And so we speak of a lee thermal.
So a thermal that's there at the end,
on the side facing away from the wind.
That happens of course mostly
when, for example, we have a north wind
in the Alps.
Because the south side of the mountains
in the Alps always warms up best.
So south-facing slopes are best.
If we now have a north wind, or a Bise,
on top of that for example,
then the wind comes from the north.
And so the lee thermal
is very pronounced.
Lee thermals usually have the property
of being more turbulent.
So lee rotors, mechanical turbulence.
You see, we're tying more and more things together.
We have mechanical turbulence here
that of course chops up the thermal.
It's a bit like a shredder
that, depending on wind strength, makes the thermal
more turbulent.
But usually the lee thermal is also
a bit stronger.
Because the air parcels in the lee,
in the wind-sheltered area,
could heat up for longer.
And so they're much warmer
than a windward thermal for example.
So if we had
the other wind direction,
then the air parcel
gets carried away by the wind again and again.
And so
the air mass on the windward side
can't heat up as long
as a lee thermal.
Now that's something
that's already a bit advanced,
but good to know.
What's important is that you know
what a lee thermal is.
Now we move on
from the temperature state curve —
that's really important —
of the air mass that rises,
and we're talking about the air mass
that just sits there,
which formed during the night,
just the state of the whole air mass
that lies over Switzerland at this moment.
And from this point on
we really only talk about the air mass
that's sitting there, not about the thermal any more.
The air mass can be various things.
Ideally, or normally,
the warm air mass is at the bottom
and it gets colder and colder
the higher you go.
For example with an unstable stratification.
You can imagine that
when the hot-air balloon rises,
the temperature advantage
can be sustained for much longer,
because it's getting colder above,
than if we had the opposite,
the so-called inversion.
There are, with certain weather situations,
with high-pressure influence for example,
inversions,
the other way round,
where we often have cold at the bottom
and on the mountaintops we have very warm.
You sometimes get that in winter,
when there are strong inversions.
In Zurich by the lake it's cold,
you sit in the fog down below,
and on the peaks above, while skiing
it's the most beautiful, warmest weather,
where you can almost sit out at a table.
That's a so-called inversion.
And now you can imagine,
when the thermal rises up into an inversion,
where it often gets warmer with altitude,
we no longer have any temperature advantage.
After that the air mass basically stops rising.
And so we talk about
a very stable stratification.
That's a stable stratification,
with too little thermal activity to expect.
Very calm air masses too.
You get really milky conditions.
Beautiful for hike-and-fly and so on,
but not so beautiful for thermal flying.
Down below there's the middle range,
a so-called isothermal layer,
as it's called.
That's when the temperature state
stays exactly the same with altitude.
If we have 6 degrees in the valley below
and 6 degrees up on the mountain,
then that's a so-called isothermal layer,
a constant air stratification.
Exactly.
But the temperature state in the atmosphere
is definitely such that it's warm below
and gets colder with altitude.
Now there are a few exam questions here
about this inversion
or unstable air stratification etc.,
where you'll see a temperature gradient
written out in text form —
at 500 metres it's 15 degrees,
at 800 metres it's 14 degrees etc.
It's listed out like that.
And now of course you'll often be asked,
where is there a surface inversion?
Then you have to think:
I'm looking down here at 500 metres,
and an inversion, ah,
is a reversed air stratification.
That means it gets warmer with altitude.
Then you just have to read the numbers here.
For example, here 1 is 15 degrees
and at 800 metres it's 16 degrees.
That's getting warmer, that's an inversion.
The same with a high-altitude inversion,
the same thing takes place,
just at higher altitudes.
Or an isothermal layer,
for example here at 4, where we have 8 degrees
at both altitudes —
that's a constant air stratification.
So that's an example exam question.
Then we come to the next chapter,
which is important.
Here we open up the chapter on
cloud names, cloud shapes,
cloud pictures, and what that means for us.
With clouds it's like this —
as in this diagram,
there are lots of Latin names
and it's sometimes very complicated
to remember and to know what they mean.
It's a real maze.
Now we'll try to sort it out
and see how it comes about.
For cloud names,
in my head I make
a 2-by-3 grid
and then there's also a box
for special clouds
that don't fit into this grid,
but you can imagine a 2-by-3 grid.
Then fundamentally there are two types
of cloud families.
One is the cumulus cloud,
a cumulation,
a cumulus point,
an accumulation of something —
these are heap clouds.
Heap clouds is what they're called,
everything that has a cumulus-cloud form.
Then there's stratus,
which is a layer cloud.
Everything that's fog, for example,
is a proper layer cloud.
Everything that's so nicely spread out
in horizontal cloud fields.
The two cloud families differ
mainly in that cumulus clouds
form primarily
when very strong
vertical air-mass movements
take place, so when a thermal rises
for example, or when in high
air layers an air layer is forced
to rise.
Then you get cumulus cloud forms.
Cumulus cloud forms.
With stratus it's the opposite.
That actually points more to
a stable air mass,
where there's little vertical motion in it,
and rather a horizontal cloud,
that just settles in a bit.
Just for understanding,
what happens with these two cloud forms.
On the side we have
storeys,
the various altitudes of clouds.
There we have cirro-clouds.
Ice crystals.
That's a very high altitude.
They basically only consist of ice crystals.
Then we have the alto storey.
That's a high cloud layer.
That's roughly between 4 and 7 kilometres.
And then we have the ground level,
where actually you only have the basic cloud families.
You'll see in a moment
where actually no additional name is added.
The altitudes
are always relative.
They vary a bit.
They might be 3 kilometres
or 8 kilometres. It's not fixed.
It's not a precisely defined altitude.
But what's important is that
alto clouds are really clearly above
2000 to 3000 metres.
And cirro clouds are really above 8 kilometres.
So very, very high clouds.
And now we can actually just start
putting the names of these
different clouds together.
You see, you just have to combine them.
There's cirrocumulus.
That's made up of these two names.
Then there's cirrostratus.
That's a milky soup
that's very, very high up.
That's usually what gives you a halo.
So a circular rainbow
at very high altitude.
That's cirrostratus.
That's a layer cloud again.
Then we have altocumulus
and altostratus.
Down below we have cumulus and stratus.
Those are then just the basic forms.
That's also important.
Cumulus and altocumulus differ
mainly in that cumulus
forms through sun exposure.
That's really the thermal updraft,
as we just discussed.
When a colder air layer
pushes underneath,
then the warm air layer starts to rise away
and forms small cumulus clouds.
Everything on the left-hand side
points to an unstable air stratification.
The air is very eager to rise.
There we have unstable, stable again.
Everything on the right we tend to call
a stable air stratification.
So the air isn't very eager to rise.
And we tend to find
more inversions
with stratus clouds.
Those are the two cloud families.
They get combined
and built up from these two.
It's very simple,
how it can be sorted into this grid.
Then of course we still have a few special clouds.
We have a few special cases too.
One cloud in the stratus range
is the so-called nimbostratus.
Nimbus stands for rain.
So layer clouds
that really rain themselves out.
We often have this with a warm front
or in a low-pressure area,
where it's just chucking it down,
where it's just raining.
That's usually a nimbostratus.
So precipitation.
Then we have cirrus clouds,
so without an addition.
Cirrus clouds are feather clouds.
These are very high, classic feather clouds.
For us, for flying, they're not so relevant either.
For us, for flying, that's also not so relevant.
Then we have lenticularis,
a really interesting cloud,
which we'll get into now.
And for us one of the most important clouds
is the CB, or cumulonimbus.
That's the fully developed
thunderstorm cloud.
It flashes and thunders and rains
and for us is really the most dangerous.
It extends really
from the lower storey
up to the tropopause.
We'll come to that in a moment.
And logically there are also
lots of special clouds
and detailed names
you can geek out about.
But for you, for normal flying,
what matters is just the basics — the rest doesn't really matter.
We don't need to go any deeper into it.
On the topic of clouds
there's also the degree of cloud cover,
which you read again and again in the weather report
or in gliding
in the weather report etc.
There we always talk about eighths.
So 8 eighths cover
is a closed cloud cover.
We don't see any blue sky,
it's all closed up.
0 eighths, logically no clouds,
maybe only blue thermals,
if there are any thermals at all.
There could also be blue thermals there —
we've tied it together again.
2 eighths is covered,
2 eighths of the sky are covered
and the other 6 eighths
are blue and clear.
And of course 4 eighths is half-cloudy.
4 eighths is actually a nice cloud cover
for us for thermal flying.
Something between 3 and 4 eighths,
because we have nice cumulus clouds
and here we still have enough blue sky,
so the sun can heat up the ground,
so we have nice weather,
so the thermals develop.
That's the degree of cloud cover.
A really interesting cloud form,
lenticularis,
is probably the one most pilots know,
or that you've quickly seen at some point.
Lenticularis actually needs
two or three ingredients.
One is a mountain range,
another is a very strong wind.
Lenticularis clouds always indicate
a very, very strong wind.
And of course we need
a bit of humidity,
so it can also start to condense.
That happens usually either
directly over the mountain peaks
or on the lee side of the mountain range.
These are rotors
that put the air into a wave motion
and so always,
at the top of this wave motion,
the rising air mass condenses,
such a cloud forms,
behind it the air sinks again,
it stops condensing
and then you get these wave clouds.
What's important for us to know about flying:
this is a very strong wind,
but because the mountain range
always stays in the same place
and the waves accordingly
always stay in the same place,
this is important for us.
Just because a cloud stays in place
doesn't mean there's no wind.
Logically.
But you certainly know these cloud forms
very well by sight
and for all paraglider pilots
they're always an alarm signal,
when you see lenticular clouds.
Then we have a really important topic,
which is the wind.
That's a really important topic,
which is the wind.
Then we have a really important topic
for paraglider pilots,
which is thunderstorms.
With thunderstorms it's like this:
a thunderstorm is nothing other
than a cumulus cloud
that has grown and keeps growing.
It's a really big,
nasty thermal cloud.
That's what makes it just as
dangerous for us,
because of course we want to fly thermals.
We fly precisely on the days
on which, under certain conditions,
it keeps flying further.
We have to be careful
when the thunderstorm clouds get too big.
In the morning you see the first cumulus clouds,
we go flying,
by 11 everything's nice,
wonderful flying conditions.
Then the thunderstorm clouds
or cumulus clouds get a bit bigger.
They form of course with the sun.
We always need the engine of the sun.
The engine slowly starts firing.
It's like an engine
that slowly gets going.
We have to slowly get careful,
when the thunderstorm clouds start
to merge.
We have small little clouds here
and suddenly two clouds
merge together.
But we're already talking about large areas,
not a little puffy cloud,
but large clouds
that span a few kilometres.
As soon as the cloud base
starts to merge,
we have to be careful.
Especially when the cloud base
also slowly gets dark.
You can imagine
that because of the water droplets,
and at some point, when the cloud gets tall,
the sun's rays almost can't get through any more.
That's the point where the cloud slowly
starts to appear dark,
where it's also dark under the base.
And that's the point
where you have to slowly start paying attention
and which of course has a lot to do with experience.
How much longer am I flying?
How long do I stay under this cloud?
Is this already a big cloud or not?
That's really an area
where you as an advanced pilot
need a lot of caution.
And another basic rule is,
when all of a sudden everything starts lifting,
now we're flying in the morning,
it was always hard
to find the thermal,
the local updrafts.
And then suddenly everything starts lifting.
Everywhere out in the valley I can fly
and it's lifting and up there is a big cloud.
There you really have to start being careful.
Because then the door
of this thunderstorm slowly starts to run.
And at some point more and more merge
and they get taller and taller.
And it actually doesn't even need any more energy
from the sun,
because the engine immediately starts turning.
Because warm air rises,
cools down, sinks back down.
Because we have cold air,
the warm air just rises faster
and then you get a kind of chain reaction
and a turbo that starts firing.
And the cloud doesn't always grow,
but often up to the tropopause.
And then you get these beautiful
anvil shapes of the cloud.
That's when, when the thunderstorm cloud rises to the top,
you can imagine,
like a Perspex sheet
that lies over it, where the cloud grows up to it
and then can only go sideways
and forms the anvil.
And in this phase, when the thunderstorm is fully developed,
there's a lot of friction in the air mass,
which sinks and rises.
And so sometimes there's lightning
and there's mostly also precipitation.
Uhm, that's it for thunderstorms.
And something I'd really like
to lay on your heart,
everyone is afraid of the thunderstorm
when they're under it.
So the climb rates are extreme,
you really have to be careful there.
Uhm, but what's even more concerning
is the danger
from the outflow of a thunderstorm like that.
The air mass that rises and then
extremely cools down up at 13,000
metres up there,
at some point sinks down
and it's like a bucket of water that gets dumped out.
And accordingly
the cold air empties out
and it's heavy
and cold, so it heads for the ground.
And it can really dump out
and then it goes into the valleys
and down to the landing zones
and it can also cause very strong turbulence
much further away from the thunderstorm.
So that's not only the danger
directly under the thunderstorm,
but really a huge danger
next to the thunderstorm too.
Because the cold-air outflows really go in down below
and that makes the landing extremely turbulent
and dangerous
and very, very unpleasant.
And the cold air or outflows can then,
under certain circumstances, also trigger the next thunderstorm,
where then warm air masses again —
which, for example, are sitting at the valley floor —
the cold air mass comes down,
the warm rises like the weather and then gives
the next turbulence
and maybe even the thunderstorm.
So you really have to be careful
about these cold-air outflows of the thunderstorm.
It's almost more dangerous than the updraft.
So the updraft is also dangerous logically,
but the cold-air outflows are the ones
that unfortunately many pilots underestimate again and again.
When you look at it, it looks like this.
When you see precipitation
under a cloud, then it's the last —
the last,
it's the ultima ratio, to land
or to fly as far away as possible
and then land as soon as possible.
But of course when it looks like this, then it's really no longer good

You can really see how the bucket of water

cold air gets let out below.

Then again, the radar, as we've already said.
It's important — look at the radar,

Look at the cloud below.
If they're clouds, you don't see how high they are up top.
But you might see another cloud

the cloud everywhere isn't much smaller either.
Then with the radar it's really important —
look at the radar, that's a good thing.
But also, keep your eyes

sometimes also not exactly what

So always keep your eyes
open and look at what's happening all around.
And what it looks like.
And if it's a medium-sized cloud,
maybe also a little tip.
If it's not yet a thunderstorm,

is it already sucking, is it still good or not,

Then you can quickly always get out of

fly along the edge of the cloud. But obviously
with thunderstorms really be careful.
That has a lot to do with know-how.

The
valley-wind system.

we have again — you see,

And so the warming
in the alpine region produces
a
lower air pressure over
the course of the day. And as we

We have higher pressure
in the lowlands
and lower pressure
in the mountains. And so
there's an equalising flow
over the course of the day.
Careful, that's not to be confused with a

And so there's the equalising flow
over the course of the day.
It's usually strongest,
starting around 11 it kicks in,
it reaches its peak
around 3 in the afternoon.
And in terms of the season, it's like this:
in spring, as soon as
the inner-alpine region
slowly becomes snow-free,

Then it runs through into autumn.
It gets weaker and weaker in summer

Of course if we have an overcast sky

And so at night
you have the opposite.
When a mountain wind sets in,

around 6 it kicks in.
Then we often have higher pressure
in the alpine region, because the mountains

You might know the lowlands.
If you sit by the lake, it stays much longer

also again have a pressure difference,
where we have higher pressure in the lowlands,

an equalising flow, which around 6

And there's the phenomenon

There's a kind of reverse thermal,

at a special spot, for example Interlaken,

There are special weather phenomena.
But that's the mountain wind that sets in.

The valley wind can really become a problem in spring,

you can imagine, like a root

The further into the main valley you are,

the more valley wind we have,

Sometimes you can get 50–60 km

Up top, the valley wind, that's only in the valley,

at 500 metres above the ground, depending.

That's something you really have to watch out for,

There are also still some special phenomena

So it can be that you

exactly the same. So a feeling

how much of a wind system,
as we've had it, a pressure equalisation.
We need a pressure difference,
so we need two ingredients for Foehn.
We need a pressure difference
and we need a big mountain range.
That is to say, Foehn

It's in the Himalayas, in the Andes,
in the Pyrenees
and here in the Alps.
The Foehn has a different name in each place;

For us now on the northern side of the Alps,
of course the South Foehn is the one that causes problems.

We have higher pressure on the southern side of the Alps
than on the northern side.
Accordingly,
the air mass wants to equalise the pressure
and flows
over the mountain ridge.
With Foehn maybe still important is,
that depending on the situation
the Foehn gets really sucked in
from the lowlands. You can imagine,

And this air
really wedges into the valleys

and can cause very, very strong winds
down in the valley. There are sometimes special
weather situations where there isn't always much wind
at the take-off, but in a Foehn valley
there's extremely strong wind.
Of course that's what makes it dangerous for us on the northern side of the Alps,
because with Foehn
we usually have lots of sun,
beautiful weather, blue skies, everything is nice,
it would actually be super beautiful for flying,
it would also always be really tempting.
But that's also what makes it just as dangerous with Foehn,
that it comes about precisely in such nice-looking weather.
It comes about, in such a nice weather pattern.
And specifically the air mass rises
in this case on the southern side of the Alps,
it condenses —
we have it again, the air mass rises,
it condenses, forms a cloud,
under certain circumstances it even rains out

Alpine ridge, forms a so-called
Foehn wall — that's really like a wall
that follows the main Alpine ridge.
It wedges down,
dries out,
it dissolves the clouds and the dry
warm wind wedges into the lowlands,
into the Alpine valleys
and of course causes very, very strong

Foehn can sometimes be dry
